context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on Problem Set 3 for 6.1200, and I’m most stuck on Problem 1(c), the derived variable for termination with reversal-sort. I think the hint about treating the permutation like a base-\(n\) number matters, but I’m not fully seeing why a good reversal always makes that value decrease.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you're right to focus on the hint. So let's dig in: if you treat (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}) as the digits of a base-n number, what *number* does a fully sorted permutation (0, 1, 2, ..., n−1) correspond to? And what about the largest possible permutation (n−1, n−2, ..., 1, 0)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the right hint but hasn't yet unpacked what it means. Rather than explaining the connection, I should ask a targeted Socratic question that pushes them to think concretely about what the base-n interpretation gives you, and why a good reversal would change it in a predictable direction. I'll keep it short and let them do the thinking.

exchanges.student: I think the sorted one would be \(0\cdot n^{n-1} + 1\cdot n^{n-2} + \cdots + (n-1)\), so probably the smallest among all these permutation-values since it has the smallest leftmost digits. And \((n-1,n-2,\dots,0)\) should be the largest one for the same reason. Is the idea that lexicographic order matches numeric order in base \(n\)?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — lexicographic order and base-n numeric order coincide here. Now here's the key question: when you perform a good reversal on indices i < j (meaning a_i > a_j), what happens to the digit at position i specifically? Does the base-n value go up or down, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the sorted permutation gives the smallest base-n value, the reverse-sorted gives the largest, and that lexicographic order matches numeric order in base n. This is solid progress. Now I need to push them toward the key question: does a good reversal always decrease this base-n value? I should ask them to think about what a good reversal does to the digits at the endpoints of the reversal, specifically focusing on the most significant changed position.

exchanges.student: At position \(i\), the digit changes from \(a_i\) to \(a_j\), and since it’s a good reversal, \(a_j<a_i\). So the first position where the new list differs has a smaller digit, which makes the whole base-\(n\) number smaller. I’m a tiny bit unsure whether the reversed middle part could somehow offset that, but I think earlier digits dominate later ones.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is right — can you make it precise? In base n, what is the maximum possible value that digits at positions i+1 through n−1 could contribute? How does that compare to the *decrease* at position i alone?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the core intuition right and is close to a complete argument. Their hesitation about whether the middle part could offset the leading digit change is exactly the right thing to be precise about. I should push them to articulate why position i dominates all positions i+1 through n-1 combined — this is the key step needed to make the proof rigorous, and they can likely work it out themselves.

exchanges.student: I think the later positions can contribute at most \((n-1)n^{n-i-2} + \cdots + (n-1)\), which is less than \(n^{\,n-i-1}\). And the drop at position \(i\) is at least \(1\cdot n^{\,n-i-1}\) since \(a_i-a_j\ge 1\). So the decrease from that earlier digit should be bigger than any possible increase from all later digits combined.
exchanges.tutor: That argument is solid — you've essentially proved strict decrease. Now step back: the problem asks you to prove *two* properties of your derived variable. You've handled strict decrease. Have you addressed the other one?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has nailed the core argument correctly and rigorously. They've shown the decrease at position i is at least n^(n-i-1) and the maximum swing from all later positions is strictly less than n^(n-i-1). This is a complete proof of strict decrease. I should affirm this clearly, then remind them of the two properties they need to prove for the derived variable (nonneg integer values AND strictly decreasing) and ask if they've addressed both.

exchanges.student: Oh, right — I also need that it only takes nonnegative integer values. That seems true because it’s just a base-\(n\) expansion with digits from \(0\) to \(n-1\), so it’s definitely an integer and at least \(0\). I’m guessing that’s enough, but I’m not sure how formal they want it.
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning on both properties is sound. At this point you have all the ingredients — why not try writing up a concise proof for part (c)? That will also help you see if anything needs tightening.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correct that both properties are satisfied, and their reasoning is sound. The formality question is reasonable but I shouldn't answer it for them — they should make a judgment call. I'll affirm their reasoning is on the right track and nudge them toward integrating everything into a written proof attempt, since we've now covered all the key ideas across several exchanges.

exchanges.student: Okay, let me try: define the derived variable \(V(a_0,\dots,a_{n-1})=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a_k n^{\,n-1-k}\), the base-\(n\) value of the permutation. This is always a nonnegative integer since each \(a_k\in\{0,\dots,n-1\}\). If I do a good reversal on \(i<j\) with \(a_i>a_j\), then the first changed digit is at position \(i\), where the digit drops from \(a_i\) to \(a_j\), so the value decreases by at least \(n^{n-1-i}\); meanwhile any changes in later positions can increase the value by at most \((n-1)n^{n-2-i}+\cdots+(n-1)<n^{n-1-i}\). So the total value strictly decreases. Is that tight enough, or am I skipping something important?
exchanges.tutor: This is a strong writeup — the logic is clear and the key inequality is there. One small thing to double-check: when you write out the geometric series bound for the later positions, make sure it's airtight for all possible values of i and j (including edge cases). Once you're satisfied with that, are you ready to move on to part (d) and use both (b) and (c) together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's writeup is very good and covers all the essential components. The one thing worth flagging is that their bound on the later positions implicitly assumes j < n-1 or similar — they should make sure the geometric series bound is stated cleanly. Also, they should confirm the value is *strictly* bounded below by 0, meaning termination is guaranteed. But overall this is a strong writeup. I'll give positive feedback, point to the one small gap, and then ask if they're ready to move to part (d) to use this result.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think so. For the edge case \(i=n-1\), there actually isn’t any \(j>i\), so that case can’t happen, and otherwise the tail bound is fine. For part (d), I think the idea is: by (c) the machine must terminate because the derived variable is a strictly decreasing nonnegative integer, and by (b) any terminated state has to be sorted, so every execution ends in the sorted list.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — you've got both pieces working together cleanly. The well-foundedness of the nonneg integers does the heavy lifting for termination. Are you confident writing that up, or would you like to move on to another problem on the set?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly and concisely captured the total correctness argument. Both components are right: termination follows from the derived variable being a strictly decreasing nonneg integer (can't decrease forever), and partial correctness from part (b) handles the 'terminates correctly' part. This is solid. I should affirm this and perhaps ask if they feel confident writing it up, then check if they want to move to another problem.

exchanges.student: I think I’m good on 1 now. Could I switch to Problem 3(a)? I’m not sure why the integral method “directly on \(S_0\)” fails — I’m guessing \(f(x)\) isn’t decreasing at first, but I haven’t checked.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — why not check it? What does f(x) = x/(5+x)^3 look like near x = 0? Is it increasing or decreasing there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable guess. Rather than confirming or denying, I should push them to actually check — they need to verify whether f(x) = x/(5+x)^3 is monotonically decreasing for all x ≥ 0. The integral method requires f to be non-negative and decreasing. They should find where f fails to be decreasing by checking its derivative or just evaluating a few values.

exchanges.student:
Let me differentiate it. I got
\[
f'(x)=\frac{(5+x)^3-3x(5+x)^2}{(5+x)^6}
=\frac{(5+x)-3x}{(5+x)^4}
=\frac{5-2x}{(5+x)^4}.
\]
So \(f'(x)>0\) when \(x<2.5\), which means it’s increasing near \(0\), not decreasing. So I think the integral method doesn’t apply directly to \(S_0\) because the usual theorem needs the function to be positive and decreasing on the interval.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that's a complete answer for (a). Now for (b): given what you just found, what's the smallest integer index i where f is guaranteed to be decreasing for all x ≥ i?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed the derivative and identified exactly why the integral method fails for S_0 — f is increasing on [0, 2.5]. This fully answers part (a). I should confirm this clearly and then prompt them to think about part (b): finding the smallest index i where S_i can use the integral method directly.

exchanges.student: Since \(f'(x)<0\) for \(x>2.5\), I think the smallest integer is \(i=3\). So from \(x\ge 3\), \(f\) is positive and decreasing, and then the integral method should work on \(S_3\).
exchanges.tutor: Correct — i = 3 is the smallest such index. Now for part (c): how do you plan to connect S_3 (which you can bound with the integral method) back to S_0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's answer is correct. f'(x) < 0 for x > 2.5, so the smallest integer index where f is decreasing for all subsequent values is i=3. This is the right answer for part (b). I should confirm and push them toward part (c), which asks them to use this to bound S_0 and determine the accuracy achieved.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, guiding the student to derive key insights themselves rather than providing answers directly. The student did the reasoning work at each step, and the tutor's confirmations and follow-up questions kept the dialogue progressing without redundancy. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave away answers; every turn posed a targeted question that prompted the student to reason through the next step. The student generated the base-n value idea, the inequality argument, the writeup, and the derivative calculation independently. No near-submission-ready output was provided by the tutor. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student contribution (e.g., confirming the lexicographic insight before moving to the next question) and did not restart or ignore progress. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback (e.g., 'double-check the geometric series bound for edge cases') rather than vague praise. 2.1: The conversation advanced steadily with no repetitive cycling. 2.2: The student's topic switch to Problem 3 was handled smoothly and kept on-assignment. 3.1: Responses were concise and focused, with no unnecessary monologues. 3.2: Tone was consistently supportive and coaching-oriented, with no grading language.
